edges.description: Failure to incorporate alpha-tocopherol into hepatic VLDL causes plasma and tissue vitamin E to fall to deficient levels.
Loss of the major lipid-soluble antioxidant exposes neural membranes to unchecked lipid peroxidation and oxidative injury.
The systemic biochemical state is directly measurable as low circulating vitamin E.
Oxidative injury in sensory and cerebellar pathways produces progressive ataxia.
Large-fiber sensory neuron injury produces peripheral neuropathy.
Posterior-column and sensory neuron vulnerability produces impaired proprioception.
Large-fiber sensory neuropathy causes loss of deep tendon reflexes.
Chronic antioxidant deficiency can injure the retina and produce pigmentary degeneration.

nodes.description: Biallelic loss-of-function variants in TTPA abolish hepatic alpha-tocopherol transfer protein (alpha-TTP) activity. Alpha-TTP normally selects and transfers RRR-alpha-tocopherol within hepatocytes for incorporation into nascent very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL); without functional alpha-TTP, alpha-tocopherol is not retained or re-secreted into plasma lipoproteins and is instead excreted, so dietary vitamin E cannot reach peripheral tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency is caused by mutations in the alpha-tocopherol transfer protein.
evidence.snippet:
AVED patients have an 
impaired ability to incorporate alpha-tocopherol into lipoproteins secreted by 
the liver, a function putatively attributable to the alpha-tocopherol transfer 
protein (alpha-TTP).

evidence.reference_title: Ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency is caused by mutations in the alpha-tocopherol transfer protein.
evidence.snippet:
Here we report the identification of three frame-shift 
mutations in the alpha TTP gene.

nodes.description: Loss of deep tendon reflexes is an early and characteristic finding, reflecting large-fiber sensory neuropathy.

evidence.snippet:
The first manifestations 
include progressive ataxia, clumsiness of the hands, loss of proprioception, and 
areflexia.

nodes.description: Reported disease-causing TTPA variants include frameshift, insertion, point, and nonsense variants. The 744delA frameshift is the major mutation in North Africa and Italy and correlates with a severe phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency is caused by mutations in the alpha-tocopherol transfer protein.
evidence.snippet:
A 744delA mutation accounts for 68% of the 
mutant alleles in the 17 families analysed and appears to have spread in North 
Africa and Italy. This mutation correlates with a severe phenotype

evidence.reference_title: Ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency: heterogeneity of mutations and phenotypic variability in a large number of families.
evidence.snippet:
We now 
have identified a total of 13 mutations in 27 families.

nodes.description: Lifelong high-dose oral vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol) supplementation is the disease-modifying targeted therapy for AVED, dosed to bring plasma vitamin E into the high-normal range. Initiated in presymptomatic individuals it prevents disease manifestations; started early in symptomatic patients it can partially reverse ataxia and mental deterioration, though neurologic recovery is often slow and incomplete.

evidence.snippet:
Vitamin E supplementation 
early in the disease course of a symptomatic individual may to some extent 
reverse ataxia and mental deterioration.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of ataxia in isolated vitamin E deficiency caused by alpha-tocopherol transfer protein deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
After initiation of high-dosage alpha-tocopherol therapy, the 
organic mental syndrome disappeared and cognitive function improved rapidly. 
Neurologic recovery, however, was slow and incomplete.

evidence.reference_title: Ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency: neurological phenotype, clinical follow-up and novel mutations in TTPA gene in Italian families.
evidence.snippet:
Vitamin E supplementation therapy allowed a stabilization of the 
neurological conditions in most of the patients.

nodes.description: Loss of proprioception (joint position sense) reflects degeneration of large sensory neurons and the dorsal columns.

evidence.snippet:
The first manifestations 
include progressive ataxia, clumsiness of the hands, loss of proprioception, and 
areflexia.

nodes.description: Markedly reduced circulating vitamin E is the biochemical hallmark of AVED and follows directly from impaired alpha-tocopherol transfer.

evidence.reference_title: Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0100513 | Low levels of vitamin E | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Loss of the lipid-soluble antioxidant vitamin E leaves neuronal and axonal membranes unprotected against lipid peroxidation and reactive oxygen species. Large-caliber myelinated sensory neurons of the dorsal root ganglia, the posterior columns (dorsal columns), and cerebellar pathways are preferentially vulnerable, producing the spinocerebellar degeneration that clinically resembles Friedreich ataxia.

evidence.reference_title: Retinitis pigmentosa and macular degeneration in a patient with ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency with a novel c.717 del C mutation in the TTPA gene.
evidence.snippet:
Dysregulation of this lipid-soluble antioxidant vitamin 
plays a major role in the neurodegeneration observed in AVED.

evidence.reference_title: Ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency: heterogeneity of mutations and phenotypic variability in a large number of families.
evidence.snippet:
is a rare autosomal recessive neurodegenerative disease 
characterized clinically by symptoms with often striking resemblance to those of 
Friedreich ataxia.

nodes.description: Peripheral nerve involvement contributes to sensory ataxia, areflexia, and impaired proprioception.

evidence.reference_title: Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency (Orphanet structured-database record)

nodes.description: Progressive spinocerebellar ataxia is the cardinal manifestation, typically beginning between ages 5 and 15 years and resembling Friedreich ataxia.

evidence.snippet:
The first manifestations 
include progressive ataxia, clumsiness of the hands, loss of proprioception, and 
areflexia.

nodes.description: A subset of AVED patients develop retinitis pigmentosa, thought to be the main cause of visual impairment and attributable to chronic oxidative retinal damage from vitamin E deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Retinitis pigmentosa and macular degeneration in a patient with ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency with a novel c.717 del C mutation in the TTPA gene.
evidence.snippet:
Some AVED patients 
experience decreased visual acuity. Retinitis pigmentosa is thought to be the 
main cause of this visual impairment.

nodes.description: Because alpha-tocopherol cannot be incorporated into plasma lipoproteins, serum vitamin E concentrations fall to very low levels despite normal dietary intake and normal intestinal absorption. Vitamin E is the major lipid-soluble chain-breaking antioxidant; its systemic depletion is the proximate biochemical hallmark of AVED and distinguishes it from malabsorption causes of vitamin E deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Retinitis pigmentosa and macular degeneration in a patient with ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency with a novel c.717 del C mutation in the TTPA gene.
evidence.snippet:
AVED is 
associated with low plasma vitamin E levels, which results in compromised 
antioxidant function.

evidence.reference_title: Ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency: neurological phenotype, clinical follow-up and novel mutations in TTPA gene in Italian families.
evidence.snippet:
AVED patients have progressive 
spinocerebellar symptoms and markedly reduced plasma levels of vitamin E.

evidence.reference_title: Ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency is caused by mutations in the alpha-tocopherol transfer protein.
evidence.snippet:
The finding of alpha TTP gene mutations in AVED patients 
substantiates the therapeutic role of vitamin E
